coredata.dc:title: Pronounced interfacial interaction in icosahedral Au@C60 core-shell nanostructure for boosting direct plasmonic photocatalysis under alkaline condition
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Yue
Cui, Qingqiang
Xu, Linlin
Jiao, Anxin
Li, Shuang
Wang, Xuelin
Chen, Ming
coredata.dc:description: The unique hot carrier-driven direct plasmonic photocatalysis of coinage metal nanomaterials (NMs) via energetic localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) in visible-light region has been explored in recent years. However, the low photoinduced electron transfer efficiency and insufficient separation of electron-hole pairs would severely preclude their widespread practical applications. Herein, we demonstrate an interesting plasmonic photocatalyst based on the construction of icosahedral (Ih) Au@C60 core-shell NMs, taking advantage of specific delocalized π electrons structure of a tight C60 shell and enhanced LSPR property of Ih Au core. Then, the pronounced interfacial interaction at junction region endows the obtained Au@C60 NMs with an outstanding photoinduced hot carrier-transmission during photocatalytic reaction, facilitating a remarkably higher (1.89 times) photocatalytic activity toward visible-light driven degradation of crystal violet (CV) dyes, as compared to bare Au NMs. Impressively, the photocatalytic activity of Ih Au@C60 NMs can be effectively optimized by changing the pH value of reaction solution, with the kinetic rate constant reaching the maximum value of 0.179 min−1 in pH0 11.4 solution, while 0.005 min−1 at pH0 3.0. Moreover, due to the protection of a tight C60 shell, the Ih Au@C60 NMs also possess excellent photocatalytic stability/reusability in recycling runs, holding great potential for the design of robust and high-performance plasmonic photocatalysts in repeated practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Direct plasmonic photocatalysis
Icosahedral Au@C60 core-shell nanostructure
Localized surface plasmon resonance
Delocalized π electrons
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ih Au@C60 core-shell NMs
Synthesis of spherical Au@C60 core-shell NPs
LSPR effect of Ih Au@C60 NMs on photocatalytic activity
Photocurrent responses and role of reactive species
Effects of pH on photocatalysis and structures of Ih Au@C60 NMs
Stability/reusability of flexible Ih Au@C60 film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of plasmon-induced generation of hot carriers on Ih Au@C60 core-shell NMs and the mechanism of charge separation and photocatalytic process under broad-spectrum light irradiation.
(a, b) Low and enlarged magnification TEM images of spherical Au@C60 core-shell NPs. (c) The HRTEM image of an individual Au@C60 NPs.

Morphology and structural characterizations of the obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs. (a, b) SEM and (c, d) TEM and HRTEM images. (e) SAED pattern. (f) Cross-sectional compositional line profile. (g) Raman spectrum of Ih Au@C60 NMs.

(a) XRD patterns of Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and Au NPs. (b) UV–visible absorption spectra of these three samples. (c) Down-converted photoluminescence spectra of C60. (d) Spectral overlap of C60 emission and Ih Au@C60 NMs absorption spectrum, both peaks are normalized.
High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s on the surface of (a) C60, O1s on the surfaces of (b) Au NPs and (c) Ih Au@C60 NMs. (d) The detailed structure information of Au NPs and Ih Au@C60 NMs.

(a) SERS spectra of 10−7 M CV adsorbed on Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs, respectively. The excitation wavelength is 633 nm. (b) FDTD calculations of near-field distributions for Au NPs and Ih Au with excitation of 633 nm. E inc is parallel to the y axis.

(a) Temporal evolution of UV-vis absorption spectra of CV dyes during the photocatalytic degradation in the presence of Ih Au@C60 NMs under visible light irradiation. (b) Photocatalytic degradation of CV as a function of irradiation time, (c) kinetic linear simulation and (d) the corresponding rate constant (k) histogram of visible-light-driven photocatalytic performances in Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and Au NPs systems.

(a) Based on the obtained Ih Au@C60 photocatalyst, the absorption spectra of CV dyes after photocatalytic degradation of 3 h by using three laser sources with wavelength of 532, 635 and 808 nm, respectively. (b) Degradation rate of CV dyes as a function of the laser wavelength. Overlaid (black line) is the UV-visible absorption spectrum of Ih Au@C60 NMs. FDTD calculations of near-field distributions of Ih Au nanostructure with excitation of 532 nm (c1), 635 nm (c2) and 808 nm (c3), respectively. E inc is parallel to the y axis.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses of Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs in 0.1 M Na2SO4 under visible light irradiation. (b) The photocurrent density of three samples at 200 s.

(a) The influence of different capture agents on photocatalytic performance of Ih Au@C60 NMs. (b) ESR spectra of the DMPO–•OH recorded on Ih Au@C60 NMs, bare Au NPs and original C60 powders under visible light irradiation.
(a–c) Influence of pH0 on the photocatalytic degradation of CV dyes in the presence of Ih Au@C60 NMs. (d) High resolution XPS spectra of O1s region on the surface of Ih Au@C60 at pH0 11.4.

Recycling runs of photocatalytic degradation of CV dyes under visible light irradiation at pH0 11.4 in the presence of Ih Au@C60 powders (a) and flexible film (c). (b) Digital photos of flexible Ih Au@C60 film and the mixed CV solution before and after a typical photocatalytic reaction. (d) The XRD pattern and (e) SEM image of Ih Au@C60 NMs after 10 cycles of repeated applications.

head.ce:title: Pronounced interfacial interaction in icosahedral Au@C60 core-shell nanostructure for boosting direct plasmonic photocatalysis under alkaline condition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Frontier and Interdisciplinary Science, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Frontier and Interdisciplinary Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute of Frontier and Interdisciplinary Science, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Physics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Shandong Jianzhu University, Jinan 250101, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Science, Shandong Jianzhu University, Jinan 250101, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unique hot carrier-driven direct plasmonic photocatalysis of coinage metal nanomaterials (NMs) via energetic localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) in visible-light region has been explored in recent years. However, the low photoinduced electron transfer efficiency and insufficient separation of electron-hole pairs would severely preclude their widespread practical applications. Herein, we demonstrate an interesting plasmonic photocatalyst based on the construction of icosahedral (Ih) Au@C60 core-shell NMs, taking advantage of specific delocalized π electrons structure of a tight C60 shell and enhanced LSPR property of Ih Au core. Then, the pronounced interfacial interaction at junction region endows the obtained Au@C60 NMs with an outstanding photoinduced hot carrier-transmission during photocatalytic reaction, facilitating a remarkably higher (1.89 times) photocatalytic activity toward visible-light driven degradation of crystal violet (CV) dyes, as compared to bare Au NMs. Impressively, the photocatalytic activity of Ih Au@C60 NMs can be effectively optimized by changing the pH value of reaction solution, with the kinetic rate constant reaching the maximum value of 0.179 min−1 in pH0 11.4 solution, while 0.005 min−1 at pH0 3.0. Moreover, due to the protection of a tight C60 shell, the Ih Au@C60 NMs also possess excellent photocatalytic stability/reusability in recycling runs, holding great potential for the design of robust and high-performance plasmonic photocatalysts in repeated practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
As for diverse photocatalytic reactions, semiconductor-based heterostructures [1,2] with controlled band-gap transition and plasmonic metallic nanostructures [3,4] with unique hot carrier (electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs)-driven photocatalysis represent two main members of photocatalysts for promoting solar-energy-conversion. In the field of traditional semiconductor-based photocatalysis, controlling morphology [5], doping ions into the lattice [6,7], coupling with metals [8], and compositing with non-metals [9–11] have been proven to be effective strategies to improve the photocatalytic performance of semiconductor component. The dominant roles of co-catalysts have been recognized to overcome the rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and the poor visible–near-infrared (NIR) light utilization to a certain extent. Among these co-catalysts, fullerene C60 as an interesting representative molecular material has been attracting increasing interest in semiconductor-based photocatalysis, owing to its superior physical/chemical properties, including non-linear optical response, excellent photoconductivity and chemical stability, as well as unique stable conversion emission luminescence, etc. [12–18]. Moreover, the three-dimension structure of C60 with specific delocalized π electrons is a good candidate for the efficient acceptance of electrons, which has been applied to the field of photocatalysis as one of the most attractive features [19]. Taking advantage of non-localized conjugated π electrons of C60, considerable efforts have been devoted to the semiconductor-C60 hybrid photocatalysts, such as C60-BiOCl [9], C60-hybridized ZnO [20], C60-TiO2 [21], C60@AgCl-LDO [22], etc. Recently, Bai et al. [23] reported that graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) decorated with C60 exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity for visible light-driven degradation of methylene blue and phenol molecules in comparison with pure g-C3N4. It has been illustrated that the improved performance is highly dependent on the valance band shift of g-C3N4 to lower energy position by incorporating C60, as well as the rapid photo-induced electron transfer rate and effective charge separation of C60 [23]. In addition, Song et al. [24] also revealed that the modified C60-Cr2-
x Fe x O3 composites exhibited excellent photocatalytic hydrogen evolution due to the intense interaction of C60 and Cr2-
x Fe x O3 with conjugative three-dimensional (3D) π-system. These previous studies have made use of the unique electronic properties and structure of C60 that can be served as an excellent electron transporter and acceptor to achieve an enhanced charge separation of semiconductor component by photoinduced electron transfer on C60. On the other hand, the wide band-gap of semiconductor photocatalyst should be also adjusted by incorporating auxiliary materials, in order to improve the utilization of solar energy by increasing its absorption of visible light [25,26]. However, the visible-light absorbance capacity is very limited by using C60 in wide band-gap semiconductor system due to the poor optical response property of C60 molecules, creating a huge barrier for efficiently broadening photocatalysts response to visible light. Up to now, although fullerene C60 is characterized by plentiful excellent physical/chemical properties, the inherent deficiency of poor visible-light adjustment is still “bottleneck” for these C60-semiconductor photocatalysts to meet the requirement of practical application.

Alternatively, compared to semiconductor, the plasmonic metallic nanomaterials (NMs), such as gold (Au), silver (Ag) and copper (Cu), served as a new family of photocatalysts, have drawn enormous attention and been widely exploited in recent years, owing to their powerful visible-light-harvesting capabilities that originated from energetic tunable localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) in visible-NIR region [27]. The strong coupling interaction between the nano-sized plasmonic metals and incident light at specific photon energies is derived from strong optical excitation of the collective electronic oscillations (called surface plasmons) on their surfaces [28]. After light absorption and LSPR excitation of the plasmonic metallic nanostructure, the hot carriers will be generated by the photon energy collected around the metallic surfaces [27,29,30]. Recently, the hot carrier-driven direct plasmonic photocatalysis has been continuously developed, since the pronounced tunable visible-light harvesting and subsequent photocatalytic reaction can be realized in one material. Especially, Mukherjee et al. [28] reported that the hot electrons formed by plasmon decay on Au NPs under visible light irradiation could facilitate the dissociation of hydrogen at the room temperature. Moreover, Roy et al. [31] proposed to introduce ligands on the surface of Au NPs to increase the possibility of the electron transfer process through electrostatic interaction with the reactants, and to achieve high-efficiency photocatalytic regeneration of nicotinamide cofactor. However, most of the previous reports on direct plasmonic photocatalysis were focused on the hot electron-induced photothermal conversion and the injection of energized charge into adsorbed reactants [28,31–33]. Actually, both the electrons and the holes generated by photo-excited plasmonic metallic NMs can participate in redox reactions, which have been confirmed by Chen et al. [34]. Moreover, it should be noted that the direct plasmonic photocatalytic efficiency on bare plasmonic metallic NMs is very low owing to the poor separation of hot electron-hole pairs and short lifetime of hot carriers [35], which has so far been limited only to some exothermic partial oxidation or selective reduction [36].

Taken together, if the plasmonic metallic NMs can be incorporated with C60, the novel nanocomposites will simultaneously possess both the structural advantage of 3D C60 π-system as an excellent electron transporter/acceptor and hot carrier-driven photocatalysis of plasmonic metallic NMs with powerful visible-light-harvesting capabilities. Therefore, it can be expected that the novel plasmonic metallic NMs/C60 NMs have ability to provide an unpredicted opportunity for optimizing the photocatalytic performance.

Herein, we report an interesting plasmonic photocatalyst based on the synthesis of icosahedral (Ih) Au@C60 core-shell nanostructure. It is different from traditional C60-based nanocomposites formed in previous studies by doping C60 into as-prepared precursors, the overgrowth of C60 shell was accompanied by the formation of Au nano-core via one-step synthesis in this work. As for the construction of Ih Au@C60 core-shell nanostructure, the C60 with plentiful oxygen-containing functional hydroxyl groups (–OH) was used as an efficient reducing agent for oriented nucleation and growth of Ih Au nanocrystals in the presence of KBr and polyvinyl pyrrolidone(PVP) severed as stabilizing/capping agents in chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) solution. In this way, a tight C60 shell can be formed on the Ih Au nano-core via the concomitant reduction between C60 molecules and Au ions. The obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs with pronounced interfacial interaction at junction region have ability to accelerate the photoinduced electron transfer efficiency and separation of hot electron-hole pairs, as compared to the bare Au NPs. Moreover, the Ih Au core with sharp corners exhibits an enhanced LSPR property in comparison with the spherical Au NPs, and the down-conversion feature of C60 allows shorter-wavelength light to also excite the LSPR of Ih Au core, broadening the visible-light excitation range. Therefore, the Ih Au@C60 core-shell NMs with improved photoinduced electron transfer/acceptance on C60 shell and enhanced LSPR property on Ih Au core provide a significant higher photocatalytic activity toward visible-light degradation of CV molecules in comparison with bare Au nanospheres and spherical Au@C60 NPs. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of Ih Au@C60 NMs can be significantly promoted under alkaline condition, since the hydroxyl radicals (•OH) is confirmed as one of main active groups in this work. The synergistic degradation effects and degradation mechanisms of CV in this combined system are discussed as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Finally, the Ih Au@C60 NMs with the protection of a tight C60 shell possess excellent photocatalytic stability/reusability in repeated experiments, which is very suitable for diverse practical applications.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) and crystal violet (CV) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The C60 powder were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co. Ltd. Potassium bromide (KBr), Bothsodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals used in the study such as p-benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO) were purchased from Macklin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ih Au@C60 core-shell NMs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 0.005 g functionalized C60 powders were diluted into 20 mL distilled water under ultrasonication for 10 min. 60 μL of HAuCl4 (0.05 M), 200 μL of KBr (0.025 M) and 0.005 g PVP (M.W. 5500) powder were added to the as-prepared C60 solution under magnetic stirring condition. The mixed solution was then aged at 30 °C in a water bath for 8 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of spherical Au@C60 core-shell NPs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.005 g functionalized C60 powders were diluted into 20 mL distilled water with a 10 min ultrasonic vibration. 150 μL of 0.05 M HAuCl4 were added into the C60 solution under magnetic stirring condition (~500 rpm). Subsequently, the mixture was maintained at 30 °C for 5 h.

The Au NPs were fabricated by laser ablation of bulk Au target in 20 mL distilled water for 30 min. The 1064 nm laser beam with pulse duration of 10 ns, 10 Hz repetition rate and 2 GW/cm2 power density were used to in this section, which was originated from a Q-switched Nd-YAG (yttrium aluminum garnet) equipment (Quanta Ray, Spectra Physics).

The products were washed in distilled water and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 10 min in an ultracentrifuge. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were obtained by using a JEOL JEM-2100F with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The morphologies of the products were also characterized by focused ion beam electron microscope (FIB, Helios G4 UC). The crystalline informations were collected by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Rigaku, RINT-2500VHF) using CuK α radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using a PHI Quantera SXM. UV-vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a UV-Vis-IR spectrometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu) and a Hitachi F-4500, respectively. The Fourier transforms infrared spectrums (FTIR) of the products were measured by a UV-Vis-NIR spectrometer (Bruker, ALPHA-T, 370–7800 cm−1). Electron spin resonance (ESR, JES-X320) signal of radicals using 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the capture reagent was recorded to analyze reactive oxygen groups. All the photoelectrochemical measurements were examined by using a standard three-electrode system with 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrumental Co., Ltd, China). The photocurrent responses of the photocatalysts as visible light on or off were measured at 0.0 V.

The SERS measurements were performed using a LabRAM HR 800 coupled with 473 and 633 nm laser-beams (the output power ~20 mW, and acquisition time ~2 s used for one spectrum) at room temperature. 5 μL sample precipitation was dropped on pure silicon wafer and dried in room temperature air to prepare the SERS substrate for measuring the Raman spectra of the original Ih Au@C60 NMs with the excitation wavelengths 473 nm. Meanwhile, the prepared substrates were immersed into 1.5 mL CV (10−7 M) ethanol solution and dried spontaneously for SERS measurements with the excitation wavelengths 633 nm.

The photocatalytic performances were evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of CV dyes under visible light irradiation (λ> 420 nm) and single wavelength (532 nm, 635 nm and 808 nm) irradiations. The simulated sunlight irradiation was carried out using 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut off filter. The light intensity was fixed at 38 mW cm−2. A certain amount of the photocatalyst was dropped into 30 mL of 5 ppm CV solution. The suspensions were transferred into a closed wide cylindrical glass vessel with appropriate height, and then magnetically stirred in dark for 30 min to get absorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalysts and CV molecules. At given irradiation time intervals, 2 mL aliquots were sampled and centrifuged to remove the nanocatalysts. The concentration of CV in supernatant was monitored by a UV-Vis-IR spectrometer. The initial pH (pH0) in reaction solution was adjusted by addition of appropriate amounts of HCl (0.1 M) or NaOH (0.1 M). To investigate the active species during the photocatalystic process, quenchers which can capture O2 •–, •OH and h+ free radicals were carried out by benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA) and ammonium oxalate (AO), respectively. For the cycling tests, 15 mL product solution was loaded on the cellulose ester film by means of vacuum filtration. During photocatalytic experiment, the flexible film was placed into 30 mL of 5 ppm CV solution for 1 h in dark to reach the equilibrium of adsorption and desorption between the photocatalysts and CV dyes before light irradiation. After the degradation process, the membrane is rinsed repeatedly with deionized water.

After adding C60 powders in HAuCl4 solution, abundant –OH groups on C60 can be served as efficient active sites and effectively reduce Au3+ ions, giving rise to the nucleation of Au nanocrystals. The morphologies of as-prepared Au@C60 core-shell NPs were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements, as shown in Fig. 1 . The low-magnification TEM image in Fig. 1(a) shows that almost all the products are well-dispersed spherical nanostructures with an average diameter of ca. 50 nm by measuring the diameters of more than 300 NPs in sight on the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. S1(a) in Supporting Information). Meanwhile, the mapping and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) results of spherical Au@C60 core-shell NPs (Fig. S1(b) and (c)) illustrated that the relative ratio of the C60 species in the final nanocomposites is about 3.6%. Moreover, the core-shell structure of spherical Au@C60 NPs is confirmed by the enlarged TEM image in Fig. 1(b), where a tight and high dense C60 layer is wrapped around the outer surface of Au NP. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image provides a typical structural detail of the cross region between Au core and C60 shell. Due to different penetration depths of the incident electron beam, the continuous C60 shell with a thickness of ca. 3 nm is observed as contrasting lighter image with Au core as the darker one. As a contrast, the bare Au NPs with similar size as Au@C60 core-shell NPs were synthesized by laser ablation of bulk Au target in the deionized water (TEM images in Fig. S2). The intuitive comparative result of two nanoproducts in Figs. 1(b) and S2(b) also clearly prove the existence of core-shell structure. The lattice-spacing distances in HRTEM image (Fig. 1(c)) are measured about 0.232 nm and 0.204 nm, attributing to the (111) and (200) plane of face-centered cubic Au crystal structure. The C60 shell exhibits an amorphous structure without any lattice fringes in Fig. 1(c). To further investigate the role of C60 in the synthesis process, the FTIR spectra of original C60 powders and as-prepared spherical Au@C60 NPs were carried out, as illustrated in Fig. S3. The results show that the bands at 1082, 1370 and 1613 cm−1 are assigned for the models of C–O bonds in epoxides groups, C–O–H bonds and C=C stretching vibration, respectively [37–39]. The relative intensities of these above-mentioned bands are nearly constant before and after reduction reaction in this work, implying the fullerene structure can be well kept during the synthesis of Au@C60 core-shell NPs. By contrast, the intensity of the strong and wide peak located at about 3306 cm−1 (a broad O–H band) exhibits a significant decrease trend when Au@C60 core-shell structure is formed. The obvious decreased intensity further reveals the involvement of these –OH groups in concomitant reduction reaction in the mixture of highly water-soluble C60 and HAuCl4 solution. It is inferred that the reducing −OH group on C60 surface as an electron donor reduces Au3+ to Au0 and finally gives rise to the nucleation of Au nanocrystals, which is consistent with many works that surface hydroxylated carbon materials assist the nucleation of noble metals [40–42].

Following the above core-shell growth pattern by using C60 as a novel reducing agent, Ih Au@C60 NMs were synthesized with the assistance of PVP and KBr that can effectively regulate the growth orientation of nucleation and control final morphology of Au cores. The low-magnification and enlarged SEM images in Fig. 2 (a) and (b) clearly reveal that the well-defined 3D shape of highly symmetric icosahedral nanoarchitectures from different angles of view, having an average particle size of 135–150 nm within a relatively sharp distribution. An individual Ih Au NP contains 20 single-crystal tetrahedral facets that are assembled together through 30 twin defects [43]. The core-shell structure of Ih Au@C60 NMs is further illustrated by the TEM images marked with red lines in Fig. 2(c) and (d). Moreover, most of them show three-fold symmetry structures that matched well with the structural model (inset in Fig. 2(d)). The thickness of C60 shell in Ih Au@C60 NMs is calculated about 2–3 nm (Fig. 2(c)), which is consistent with the result of spherical Au@C60 NPs in Fig. 1(c). The HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 2(d)) demonstrates the detail information of (111) Au planes with the lattice fringe of 0.235 nm and characteristic tetrahedral domains of the twinned structures. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of an individual Ih Au@C60 nanoparticle is manifested that most of the brighter spots can be grouped in concentric circles based on the distances from the center. The radii of these circles are 4.26, 4.90, 6.94, 8.13 and 8.47 nm−1 from inside to outside and the d-spacing values calculated from these radii are 0.235, 0.204, 0.144, 0.123 and 0.118 nm, which can be assigned to the {111}, {200}, {220}, {311} and {222} facets of metallic Au NPs, respectively. Moreover, the compositional line profiles of Au and C elements on the individual nanoparticle also reveal the existence of C60 shell in Fig. 2(f). The corresponding mapping image and EDS analysis of Ih Au@C60 NMs in Fig. S4 further confirm the Ih core-shell structure contains 2.3% C60 species. On the other hand, the Raman spectrum of Ih Au@C60 NMs excited by 473 nm laser beam in Fig. 2(g) shows the D-band (the breathing mode of κ-point photons of A1g symmetry) and G-band (the tangential stretching mode of the E2g phonon of SP2 atoms) characteristic of C60 carbon materials, which has been verified in previous works [42,44,45]. Based on the above experimental analyses, therefore, the Au@C60 core-shell nanostructure can be simply synthesized by using C60 as a novel reducing agent in HAuCl4 solution, and well-defined Ih structured Au nano-core will be achieved with the aid of PVP and KBr as protection/stabilization agent in this work.

Then, the crystallographic investigations of the obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs were performed by XRD measurements in Fig. 3 (a). The XRD patterns clearly reveal highly crystalline fcc Au phases (JCPDS: 04-0784) formed in three samples. As for Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs, the relative intensity ratio of (111) to (200) is calculated about 41.50, 4.45 and 3.08 in Fig. 3(a), respectively. It is deduced that the Ih Au nanostructure has an overwhelming diffraction peak intensity of (111) orientation owing to the texturing effect of icosahedra, which are catalytically more active than that of other low-indexed surfaces. Moreover, the negative-shift of the diffraction peaks in Ih Au@C60 NMs are also observed in Fig. 3(a) due to the expansion strain over their twin boundaries, indicating a change in lattice distances [43,46]. In addition, the optical properties of as-prepared nanoproducts and original C60 were examined by UV-vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Fig. 3(b) shows the absorption spectra of the obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs, respectively. Compared with the other two samples, Ih Au@C60 NMs show the broadest LSPR property with absorption band of ~617 nm in the visible region, due to the unique structure of icosahedron and the addition of C60 shell affecting the surrounding dielectric constant. It is indicated that the Ih Au@C60 NMs has the highest light-harvesting efficiency in visible region in comparison with spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs, which can generate more charge carriers upon the excitation of broad LSPR. In Fig. 3(c), it is clearly shown that C60 has the capacity to emit PL spectra at longer wavelength light (525–625 nm) under shorter wavelength light (390–540 nm) excitation, indicating that C60 possesses a remarkable down-conversion PL property. Interestingly, the LSPR absorption band of Ih Au@C60 NMs coincides well with the PL emission spectra of original C60 in Fig. 3(d), indicating that the C60 shell can be used as a superior shorter wavelength light harvester and a powerful energy-transfer component [11,47]. The unique optical property enables the C60 shell to improve the utilization of Ih Au core to solar in the visible region, giving rise to the production of more hot carriers by the broad LSPR property of Ih Au@C60 NMs.

In addition, the comparison results of surface compositions and chemical states of Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs were illustrated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Besides the C1s XPS peak at about 285 eV, the fully survey XPS spectrum of C60 shows the existence of O1s peak at 532 eV owing to the oxygen-containing functional groups formed on C60 surfaces (Fig. S5). The XPS peaks of O1s and C1s originated from spherical Au@C60 NPs and Ih Au@C60 NMs are more obvious than that of bare Au NPs, due to the formation of C60 shell on Au nano-core. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4 (a), the energy positions of Gaussian maxima for C1s spectrum derived from original C60 are located at 284.6 eV, 286.5 eV, 288.4 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of C–C bonds, C–O bonds in C–OH groups and C=O bonds, respectively [38,39,48]. In order to reveal the surface functional groups formed on Au@C60 core-shell surface, the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s generated from bare Au NPs and Ih Au@C60 NMs were carried out in Fig. 4(b) and (c). The XPS peaks of O1s located at 531.3, 532.1 and 532.6 eV are assigned to the C=O groups, C–O groups and O–H groups, respectively. Moreover, the O–H groups at 532.6 eV can be ascribed to the chemisorbed –OH or H2O on the surface of samples [9,49]. As illustrated in Fig. 4(d), the value of the area ratio of O–H group to the total O1s increased from 0.255 for Au NPs to 0.357 for Ih Au@C60 NMs. It is implied that the surface –OH groups on the Au@C60 core-shell samples were significantly improved by adding the amorphous C60 shell, facilitating the better photocatalytic activity [9].

As illustrated in previous works [50,51], the LSPR property of plasmonic metallic NMs is responsible for the incident light-harvesting and then the photon-energy conversion, which is of great significance for production of hot carriers in photocatalytic reaction. So, the LSPR properties of Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs were intuitively described by the surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) technology. It is well known that under irradiation of laser source, plasmonic metallic NMs with distinctive LSPR will show enhanced electromagnetic fields around their surfaces, leading to amplify the Raman signals of probe molecules adsorbed on their surfaces. Generally, the higher SERS activity indicates the stronger interaction with incident light, providing an enhanced LSPR property. In this work, the CV molecules (10−7 M) were used as probe molecules and the SERS spectra originated from three samples were collected, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). Obviously, the obtained Ih Au@C60 sample exhibits a remarkably higher SERS activity, and the corresponding Raman intensity at 1379 cm−1 is 3.01 and 1.77 times higher than that of bare Au NPs and spherical Au@C60 NPs. From the bare Au NPs to spherical Au@C60 NPs, it should be pointed that the increase of SERS activity is mainly due to the stronger interaction between the C60 shell and Au core that is suitable for the restriction of photo-excited electron resonance at core-shell junction region. Subsequently, compared with spherical Au@C60 NPs, the further improved SERS activity of Ih Au@C60 NMs is derived from the excellent configuration of the icosahedron with enhanced LSPR property, which is verified by the finite-difference-time-domain (FDTD) calculations, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The electric field distributions (ǀE/E 0ǀ) of spherical and icosahedral Au nanostructures (refer to Figs. 1 and 2 for the models) were simulated under the laser excitation with wavelength of 633 nm that consistent with the incident laser beam used in SERS experiments. Compared with spherical shaped Au in Fig. 5(b1), the icosahedral Au structure provides significantly enhanced electric field near the surface of icosahedron with sharp corners, supporting the formation of enhanced LSPR on Ih Au@C60 NMs (Fig. 5(b2)). It will provide a more efficient way to couple solar energy with chemical reactions, implying more hot carriers will be generated on the surface of Ih Au@C60 NMs.

According to the above experimental descriptions, the Ih Au@C60 NMs are expected to provide excellent photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation. To verify it, the visible light-driven photocatalytic degradation of CV dyes in the presence of Ih Au@C60 NMs was carried out in this work. The corresponding temporal evolution of absorption spectra of CV dyes is shown in Fig. 6 (a). It is demonstrated that the characteristic absorption peak of CV dyes at around 582 nm gradually decreases by increasing the irradiation time (0–120 min). It is showed that the photocatalytic degradation rate of 80.4% can be realized in 60 min irradiation, and 89.5% via 120 min reaction time. Besides, there is no new peak formed in the ultraviolet range (λ>200 nm) and the main absorption of original CV dyes at 582 nm also blue-shifts to 539 nm after photocatalytic reaction, indicating that a large number of N-demethylated intermediates should be generated and the whole conjugated chromophore structures of CV have been destroyed [52]. Moreover, photocatalytic activities of Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs were separately illustrated by comparing the absorption peak of CV dyes with that of pristine CV solution at designed reaction time. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the spherical Au@C60 core-shell nanocatalyst exhibits a higher photocatalytic activity (80.2%) in comparison with bare Au NPs (69.8%) under visible light irradiation of 120 min. It is attributed to the distinguished 3D structure of C60 shell with down-conversion PL property that can capture shorter wavelength-incident light to emit longer-wavelength light (525–625 nm), which in turn excites broad LSPR of Au core in visible region, generating more hot carriers (electron-hole pairs) on core-shell Au@C60 surfaces. Meanwhile, C60 shell with unique delocalized π electronic structure, served as an excellent electron acceptor, can also effectively improve electron-transfer ability and separation of photo-excited hot electron/hole pairs on the surface of Au core. In this way, the core-shell Au@C60 structure with increased hot carrier mobility has the ability to product more active oxygen radicals (Scheme 1), promoting a higher photocatalytic activity compared to bare Au NPs. On the other hand, compared with spherical Au@C60 NPs, the photocatalytic activity of the obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs can be further enhanced in Fig. 6(b), due to icosahedral Au nanostructure with pronounced LSPR on its rich edges and sharp vertices as well as twin defects. In order to further comprehend the kinetic evolution of photocatalytic degradation in the presence of different three samples, the pseudo first-order kinetics represented by ln(C/C 0) = kt were adopted in this work. The linear relationships between ln(C/C 0) and reaction time (t) are plotted in Fig. 6(c) and the corresponding k values calculated by using the first-order linear fit of the data are subsequently shown in Fig. 6(d). Clearly, the degradation rate constant of CV based on the Ih Au@C60 NMs exhibits a remarkably higher value of 0.017 min−1, which is about 1.42 and 1.89 times higher than that of spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs, respectively. It is suggested that the obtained Ih Au@C60 core-shell NMs with accelerated hot carrier mobility on C60 shell and pronounced LSPR property on icosahedral Au core provide a remarkably higher photocatalytic activity in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LSPR effect of Ih Au@C60 NMs on photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: To further reveal the LSPR effect of plasmonic metallic NMs on the enhancement of photocatalytic activity, mono-wavelength laser lights (532 nm, 635 nm and 808 nm) with similar power of ~650 mW were separately utilized in photocatalytic degradation of CV dyes by adding the same amount of Ih Au@C60 photocatalysts. After 3 h irradiation, the absorption spectra of CV dyes were recorded in Fig. 7 (a), in order to investigate the change of photocatalytic activity under different wavelength laser irradiation. By comparing the absorption peak with that of original one, obviously, a higher degradation rate (69.2%) can be achieved by introducing a 635 nm laser illumination that is well matched with the LSPR peak (~617 nm in Fig. 3(b)) of Ih Au@C60 NMs. By contrast, the degradation rates are calculated about 66.7% and 51.9% by using 532 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation in Fig. 7(a), because the incident light source wavelength deviates from the LSPR peak of the plasmonic metallic NMs. Then, the degradation rate with an error bar of 5% as a function of the incident source wavelength is plotted, as the three red dots shown in Fig. 7(b). Interestingly, the trend of the wavelength-dependent photodegradation rate in Fig. 7(b) is coincident with the absorption spectrum of Ih Au@C60 NMs (black curve line in Fig. 7(b)), indicating that the photocatalytic enhancement of plasmonic metallic NMs is highly dependent on their LSPR excitation. Moreover, the spatial electric-field distribution of icosahedral Au structure under different wavelength excitations (532 nm, 635 nm and 808 nm) calculated by FDTD simulation (Fig. 7(c)) also reveals that the degree of electric field enhancement is sensitive to the incident excitation wavelength, which is generated from LSPR of plasmonic metallic surface. On the other hand, it is worth noting that the degradation rate by 532 nm laser irradiation is higher than the overall line trend, which is due to the down-conversion characteristic of C60 molecules. The 532 nm source can not only directly excite LSPR of Au core, but also be effectively absorbed by C60 shell, emitting light with a wavelength of about 621 nm that is closer to the LSPR peak of Ih Au core. Therefore, the LSPR of Ih Au@C60 NMs can be excited by broad visible light range, holding a great potential for increasing the production of hot carriers in the subsequent photocatalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocurrent responses and role of reactive species
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the production of hot carriers in photocatalytic reaction, the effective transmission and separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs are also important for improving photocatalytic activity. So, the photoinduced electron transfer and separation of hot electron-hole pairs on Ih Au@C60 NMs, spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs were separately explored by the transient photocurrent responses under visible light irradiation, as the “on-off” irradiation cycles shown in Fig. 8 (a). It is well known that the higher photocurrent density is related to the higher efficient charge carrier transmission and separation during incident light irradiation. As indicated in Fig. 8(a), the photocurrent densities of core-shell nanostructures including Ih Au@C60 NMs and spherical Au@C60 NPs are significantly improved by introducing C60 shell, in comparison with bare Au NPs. Moreover, the icosahedra structure of Ih Au@C60 NMs with pronounced LSPR property also further promotes the corresponding photocurrent density, in comparison with spherical Au@C60 NPs. In detail, the variation of photocurrent density originated from three samples at 200 s is illustrated in Fig. 8(b). Clearly, it can be found that the Ih Au@C60 NMs provides a remarkably higher photocurrent density of 2.29 μA cm−2, which is about 3.1 times higher than that of Au NPs (0.74 μA cm−2). It indicates that a tight C60 shell formed on Au core can significantly promote the photoinduced electron transmission and accelerate the separation of hot electron-hole pairs at interconnected bounding region, then effectively increase the lifetime of hot carriers. It is beneficial to the generation of more reactive oxygen species for boosting photocatalytic activity. Interestingly, the Au@C60 core-shell structures exhibited a relatively slow photocurrent response, which is especially obvious when the current densities are normalized, as the enlarged image shown in inset of Fig. 8(a). Similar to the carbon-based photocatalytic composites [53], the composite electrode has a deferred response owing to the C60 as the electron receiver, which is also verified in this work. Compared with bare Au NPs, the core-shell structures exhibited a slower rising or declining current response when the light is turned on or off. It can be explained that C60 has the ability to trap and store some photo-excited hot electrons upon light irradiation and slowly release photo-generated hot electrons when the light is off. In this way, the obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs with production of more hot carriers on icosahedral Au structure give rise to a remarkably higher photocatalytic performance.

To further reveal the photocatalytic mechanism of Ih Au@C60 NMs, reactive species trapping experiments were performed by using isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ) and ammonium oxalate (AO) as radical scavengers of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide free radicals (O2 •–) and holes (h+), respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the photocatalytic degradation of CV dyes could be effectively limited by the addition of IPA and AO radical scavengers, which decreases from the pristine 89.5% to about 32.5% and 68.0%, respectively. However, the photocatalytic activity is slightly changed by adding BQ (from 89.5% to 85.2%). The comparison results clearly indicate that the •OH and h+ are the main oxidative species for photocatalytic reaction in the presence of Ih Au@C60 NMs, whereas O2 •– shows negligible effects. It should be noted that the remaining active hot holes after the photogenerated electrons transfer to C60 shell, would also react with water or OH– in the interspace between C60 molecules on Au core, leading to form numerous •OH radicals. It is a very strong non-selective oxidant for effective degradation of diverse organic dye molecules. Meanwhile, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique with DMPO was further explored to quantitatively analyze •OH radicals formed on nanocatalysts during the photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 9(b), no ESR signals were observed in all three systems when the reaction was performed in the dark. Besides, original C60 powders also have negligible ESR signals under visible light irradiation, which is consistent with its lack of photocatalytic behavior in the absence of plasmonic metallic NMs. Meanwhile, an obvious enhanced ESR signal for the DMPO–•OH group was detected on the Ih Au@C60 NMs, which is also higher than the weak ESR signal formed on Au NPs under visible light irradiation. In comparison with bare C60 or Au NPs, it is revealed that more chief reactive oxygen species (•OH) could be generated by overgrowth of a tight C60 shell on Ih Au nano-core.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on photocatalysis and structures of Ih Au@C60 NMs
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the indirect effect of OH– ions on Ih Au@C60 photocatalysis that can be easily oxidized to reactive •OH species via hot holes, the photocatalytic degradations of CV dyes at different pH solutions with time elapse were separately carried out, as shown in Fig. 10 (a–c). It can be seen that the photocatalytic degradation shows significant pH dependence in Fig. 10(a), which is sensitively changed by increasing pH0 value from 3.0 to 11.4. Impressively, the Ih Au@C60 NMs can provide a remarkably higher photocatalytic activity under alkaline condition when the pH0 is adjusted to 11.4, as compared to other lower pH conditions. For instance, the complete degradation (>99.5%) of CV dyes under pH0 11.4 condition can be achieved within 30 min, while the degradation rate is reduced to about 89.5% after 120 min reaction time as for pH0 8.5 condition. Subsequently, the degradation efficiency under acidic condition (pH0 3.0) is the lowest, reaching only 53.4% after 120 min irradiation. The obvious decrease of photocatalytic activity by reducing pH0 value of reaction can be further confirmed by the results of kinetic linear simulations between ln(C/C 0) and reaction time (t) in Fig. 10(b). Moreover, the corresponding value of kinetic rate constant (k) under different pH0 conditions is shown in Fig. 10(c), which gradually increases with an increase of pH0 value in reaction solution, and reaches a maximum of 0.179 min−1 at alkaline condition of pH0 11.4. Further increasing the pH0 value, the degradation performances change slightly, as shown in Fig. S6. The main reason of the optimal photocatalytic activity under alkaline solution is that the dissociative OH– ions in alkaline environment have been involved into the photocatalytic reaction, since abundant small-sized OH– ions [27] can diffuse to the surface of Au core through plentiful interspaces among C60 molecules. In this way, numerous reactive •OH species will be generated by the efficient oxidation of OH– ions via hot holes formed on Au cores under incident light irradiation, leading to a remarkably higher photocatalytic degradation rate of CV dyes. On the other hand, a higher pH liquid condition with more OH– ions in solution also affects the content of hydroxyl (–OH) groups formed on the surfaces of photocatalysts, which is confirmed in Fig. 10(d). Compared with Ih Au@C60 at pH0 8.5 condition in Fig. 4(c), the area ratio of O–H groups to the total O1s at pH0 11.4 environment is increased from 0.357 to 0.388, indicating that the rise of initial pH value is also beneficial to the formation of higher dense –OH groups absorbed on the photocatalysts. In this way, more catalytic active sites will be provided by the generated surface –OH groups that can be conductive to the transmission of electrons [54] and be also oxidized to yield reactive •OH species by the hot holes. It will offer an additional contribution for improving the photocatalytic performance. So, the photocatalytic activity of Ih Au@C60 NMs can be effectively optimized by adjusting the pH0 value of reaction solution.

Taken together, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of Ih Au@C60 NMs can be well summarized by the following analyses: (1) The enhanced LSPR property of icosahedral Au core in broad visible light region coupled with the down-conversion PL property (short wavelength of light could be down-converted to longer wavelength) of a tight C60 shell, gives rise to the high production of hot carriers on the surfaces. (2) The unique delocalized π bond structure of C60 endows the core-shell with pronounced photoinduced electron transfer, which can also dramatically promote the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs on plasmonic Au nano-core. (3) The high-yield •OH reactive species generated by the oxidation of OH–, –OH or H2O via abundant hot holes will play a critical role for boosting photocatalytic activity for organic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability/reusability of flexible Ih Au@C60 film
ce:section.ce:para: Besides a remarkably higher photocatalytic activity, the obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs should be also stable and recyclable under repeated practical applications. To verify it, the long-term stability/reusability of Ih Au@C60 photocatalytic system was evaluated by recycling tests in this work. As shown in Figs. 11 (a) and S7(a), the degradation rate decreased from 99.6% to 97.8% (pH0 11.4 condition) and from 89.5% to 84.0% (pH0 8.5 condition) after four cycles, respectively. A slight deactivation of photocatalytic activity is mainly due to the inevitable mass-loss of nanocatalysts during the centrifugation and rinsing processes, which will restrict the recycling runs of photocatalytic degradation. In order to avoid the mass-loss of nanocatalysts during repeated photocatalytic reactions, a flexible catalyst film formed by supporting Ih Au@C60 NMs on cellulose membrane has been achieved via vacuum filtration, which was then used for repeated photocatalytic degradation of CV dyes, as shown in Fig. 11(b1). In this way, the hybrid Ih Au@C60 film can be placed on the bottom of a glass dish beaker filling with CV liquid solution. As shown in Fig. 11(b2), the clean and colorless solution is easily obtained after photocatalytic reaction, and the flexible Ih Au@C60 film can be also taken out directly from the solution after the degradation reaction and rinsed with deionized water. The obtained Ih Au@C60 film is very suitable for repeated photocatalytic applications. As illustrated in Fig. 11(c), the degradation rate under pH0 11.4 condition remains at 98.4% of the initial cycle after ten recycling runs, which is obviously better than that of Ih Au@C60 powders in Fig. 11(a). The improved durability of flexible Ih Au@C60 film is also confirmed by the repeated photocatalytic experiments at pH0 8.5, as displayed in Fig. S7(b). Additionally, the XRD pattern and SEM image of nanocatalysts after ten recycling runs in both two mentioned pH conditions (Figs. 11(d, e) and S7(c, d)) reveal that the crystallographic structures and nano-morphologies are identical to that of initial ones, which can be well maintained after repeated applications. The excellent stability/reusability of Ih Au@C60 NMs is highly related to the protection of a tight C60 shell formed on Au core, since the active sites on plasmonic metallic surface cannot be directly exposed to the reaction solution. Finally, it is apparent that the enhanced photocatalytic activity and excellent stability/reusability make the obtained Ih Au@C60 core-shell NMs to become a promising plasmonic photocatalyst in diverse recycling practical applications.

In summary, we have demonstrated the development of an interesting plasmonic photocatalyst via the design of Ih Au@C60 core-shell NMs by using C60 molecules as the effective reducing agent in Au3+ solution. It is found that high yield hot carriers generated on plasmonic metallic surfaces can be achieved, due to the enhanced LSPR property of icosahedral Au core in broad visible light region coupled with the down-conversion PL property of a tight C60 shell that enable the short wavelength of light to be down-converted into longer wavelength. On the other hand, the C60 shells with non-localized conjugated π electronic structure provide pronounced photoinduced electron transfer/acceptance capacity, and facilitate the separation of hot electrons and holes more effectively. As expected, the obtained Ih Au@C60 NMs provides a remarkably higher photocatalytic activity toward the degradation of CV dyes under visible light irradiation, which is about 1.42 and 1.89 times higher than that of spherical Au@C60 NPs and bare Au NPs, respectively. Moreover, the main reactive •OH species can be easily modulated by changing the pH0 value of reaction solution, leading to a much higher photocatalytic activity under alkaline condition. On the other hand, the Ih Au@C60 photocatalyst with the protection of a tight C60 shell also exhibits excellent photocatalytic stability/reusability in repeated practical applications. These findings will offer a new option to design high-performance plasmonic metallic photocatalysts for promoting the utilization of solar energy in diverse photochemistry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Nos. 11905115 and 11575102), the Shandong Jianzhu University XNBS Foundation (No. 1608) and the Fundamental Research Fund of Shandong University (No. 2018JC022).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2021.02.062.
